reasoning: The question asks if the CDC will report any human-to-human transmission of highly pathogenic avian influenza H5N1 globally before 2026. The current date is December 21, 2025, leaving only 10 days in the resolution period. Multiple highly authoritative sources confirm that as of late November and early December 2025, no human-to-human transmission has been identified. This includes the CDC's own FluView surveillance report for week 47 (December 3, 2025), which states 'To date, human-to-human transmission of influenza A(H5) virus has not been identified in the United States.' Furthermore, the CDC global summary in late summer 2025 and subsequent academic reviews (like a November 29, 2025 Nature paper) confirm that no sustained human-to-human transmission has occurred. While some experts and a CDC scoping review in October 2025 argued that past asymptomatic cases mean transmission likely *can* occur in some settings, the CDC has not made an official report confirming a new human-to-human case. The baseline probability for such a rare event happening AND being reported in the remaining 10 days of the year is virtually nil. Considering the Metaculus community was at 1% on December 11, and we've verified no transmission since then, the probability should be the minimum sensible value. According to the platform constraints (0.02 to 0.98), I am selecting 0.02.
